original_hypothesis.title: Missing Bribe.addReward Function Breaks All BribeFactory Reward Management
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: BribeFactoryV3 defines a local IBribe interface that includes addReward(address), and multiple admin functions call this method: addRewardToBribe, addRewardsToBribe, addRewardToBribes, addRewardsToBribes. However, the actual Bribe contract (Bribes.sol) does NOT implement an addReward() function. All calls from BribeFactory to add reward tokens to bribes will revert. The only way to add new reward tokens is through notifyRewardAmount with a connector token, bypassing the intended admin workflow entirely.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a critical interface mismatch that renders the entire reward token management admin workflow non-functional. Admin cannot pre-register reward tokens on bribes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Local IBribe interface declares addReward(address) but Bribes.sol has no such function — all addReward calls will revert with missing function selector

preconditions: Admin attempts to add a reward token to any bribe via BribeFactory
Bribe contract is the actual Bribes.sol implementation

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: BribeFactory interface expects addReward but Bribe contract doesn't implement it
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin reward management is completely broken

operational_questions: Has any addReward call been attempted on-chain?
Are there bribe contracts that need additional reward tokens added?
Is there an alternative mechanism to register reward tokens?
validation_requirements: Search Bribes.sol for any function matching addReward selector
Attempt to call addRewardToBribe on a deployed bribe and verify revert
Verify notifyRewardAmount auto-adds connector tokens as workaround

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin tries to add a new reward token to an existing bribe via BribeFactory
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Transaction reverts because Bribe contract has no addReward function
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Admin workflow for reward token management is completely non-functional
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Only connector tokens can be added to bribes (via notifyRewardAmount auto-add)

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Reward token management admin functions are dead code
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Cannot pre-register non-connector reward tokens on bribes
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires Bribe contract upgrade or workaround via notifyRewardAmount

validation_result.impact_summary: Admin functions in BribeFactoryV3 intended to add reward tokens to bribes will always revert, breaking the reward management workflow.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `BribeFactoryV3` contract defines a local `IBribe` interface that includes the function `addReward(address)`. Multiple admin functions within `BribeFactoryV3` (`addRewardToBribe`, `addRewardsToBribe`, `addRewardToBribes`, `addRewardsToBribes`) rely on this interface to add reward tokens to deployed bribe contracts. However, the actual implementation of the bribe contract, `Bribes.sol`, does not contain an `addReward` function. Because the function selector does not exist in the target contract and there is no fallback function, any call from the factory to `addReward` will revert. This renders the entire administrative workflow for pre-registering reward tokens on bribes non-functional. The only remaining way to add a new reward token is if the token is already a registered 'connector' token, which allows it to be automatically added during a call to `notifyRewardAmount`.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible by comparing the `IBribe` interface in `BribeFactoryV3.sol` with the actual `Bribes.sol` contract. `BribeFactoryV3` makes external calls to `addReward`, but `Bribes.sol` lacks this function entirely.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The interface declares `addReward(address)` which is expected to be implemented by the Bribe contract.
The factory attempts to call `addReward` on the bribe contract. This will revert because `Bribes.sol` does not implement it.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When an admin calls `addRewardToBribe` (or any of its batch variants) in `BribeFactoryV3`, the factory executes an external call to the bribe contract using the `addReward(address)` selector. Since `Bribes.sol` does not have this function or a fallback function, the EVM reverts the transaction.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is a passive operational defect rather than an active exploit. An administrator attempts to add a new, non-connector reward token to an existing bribe contract by calling `addRewardToBribe` on the `BribeFactoryV3`. The transaction reverts. The administrator is permanently unable to add the token through the intended factory interface.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The administrative workflow for managing reward tokens on bribes is completely broken. Non-connector tokens cannot be added to bribes by the admin. This limits the protocol's ability to support diverse reward tokens without requiring them to be globally whitelisted as connector tokens.
recommended_mitigations: Implement the `addReward(address)` function in `Bribes.sol` with appropriate access control (e.g., `onlyAllowed`) to allow the factory to register new reward tokens.

Alternatively, if the design intent has shifted to only allow connector tokens or auto-registration via `notifyRewardAmount`, remove the `addReward*` functions from `BribeFactoryV3` to prevent confusion and dead code.
validation_result.replication_plan: Deploy `BribeFactoryV3` and a `Bribe` contract. Call `BribeFactoryV3.addRewardToBribe` with the deployed bribe's address. Observe the transaction revert with a missing function selector error.
